text: After the cystectomy and pelvic lymphadenectomy, the surgeon evaluates the small bowel approximately thirty-five centimeters proximal to the ileocecal valve to determine the optimal position for urethral anastomosis, checking different bowel segments for their reach to the urethra.

The surgeon marks the determined position on the bowel with a long stay suture, which is exteriorized through a 5mm laparoscopic port. A short stay suture is placed just distal for orientation. The specimen is extracted through a small mini laparotomy, and the bowel is exteriorized using the long stay suture.

The surgeon uses sixty centimeters of ileum to construct the neobladder, performing a hand-sewn small bowel anastomosis extracorporeally. An enterotomy is created fifteen centimeters from the distal end of the neobladder segment for urethral anastomosis. The bowel is reintroduced into the peritoneal cavity, and the robotic system is redocked.

The surgeon performs a continuous anastomosis between the neobladder and urethra using a double-armed threostratafix suture. The first limb of the neobladder is detubularised starting three centimeters from the distal end, extending to the midpoint of the sixty-centimeter ileal segment. Stay sutures are placed between the two walls using ovicral on a CT needle, followed by a continuous suture line with a three-oh stratafix.

The surgeon uses the fourth robotic arm to retract vicryl stay sutures while performing a continuous suture line. An additional fifteen centimeters of the ileal segment is detubularized, and the second limb of the posterior neobladder wall is sutured together. The detubularization and sewing of the walls are performed limb by limb to maintain orientation, using interrupted vicryl stay sutures to oppose and orient the bowel, followed by a continuous stratafix suture line.

The surgeon detubularizes the final fifteen centimeters of the ileal segment, leaving a three-centimeter ascending limb for future ureteric anastomosis. The posterior wall of the neobladder is completed and sutured using the same technique.
The surgeon folds the neobladder, using stay sutures to construct the anterior wall. The apex of the posterior wall is folded down to the midpoint of the anterior wall and secured with a stay suture.

The surgeon closes the anterior wall of the neobladder with two layers of continuous 3-0 Stratafix sutures, while the posterior wall is closed in a single layer. The proximity of the small bowel mesentery reduces the likelihood of leaks from the posterior wall, whereas the anterior wall is more prone to leakage, necessitating a double-layer closure.

The surgeon anastomoses the ureters to the ascending limb of the neobladder. The left ureter is delivered between the small bowel mesentery and the mesentery of the sigmoid colon, spatulated widely, and sewn with a three-oh stratific suture. A seven French bander stent is passed into the ureter and through the limb of the neobladder, exiting through the small bowel mesentery. The anastomosis is completed, and the stent ends are withdrawn through a five millimeter port in the left iliac fossa.
